literal: sharing, participation, attachment-to; from √bhaj 'to share, to partake of'
invariant_definition: *Bhakti* is participatory devotion to a real Lord. Because the root √*bhaj* means to share, partake, or belong-to, the term is wider than 'devotion' in the sense of private feeling. It includes cognition, affect, ritual address, remembrance, praise, service, and self-offering ordered toward Viṣṇu, Kṛṣṇa, Śiva, Devī, or another genuinely divine *iṣṭa-devatā* according to the school. *Bhakti* lives alongside terms such as *upāsanā*, *smaraṇa*, *kīrtana*, *arcana*, *dāsya*, *prema*, and sometimes *prapatti* or *rasa*. The main disputes concern what sort of state it is: preparatory discipline, continuous contemplative remembrance, the soul's abiding relational mode, or the highest end beyond liberation conceived as mere release. In practice it names things one does and undergoes, not just a sentiment one has. Because the deity is real for the traditions that foreground *bhakti*, the term should not be flattened into symbolism or reduced to emotional piety.

school_framing.shared_core: *Bhakti* names **the directed, affective-cognitive orientation of the self toward the supreme reality—Bhagavān, Iṣṭa-devatā, or Brahman—as a means and/or end of liberation**. Every theistic school agrees on this structural role (Nārada-Bhakti-Sūtra; *Bhakti-Rasāmṛta-Sindhu* 1.1.11). The schools differ on whether *bhakti* is preparatory (Advaita: *gauṇa-bhakti* under *adhikāra*), constitutive-of-*prapatti* (Viśiṣṭādvaita; Yāmuna, Vedānta Deśika), the *sahakāri* of *jñāna* (Śuddhādvaita), the supreme *puruṣārtha* above *mokṣa* (Gauḍīya; Rūpa Gosvāmī), or one of the *bhāvas* operating in the *liṅga* (Sāṅkhya, *Sāṅkhya-Kārikā* 23).
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same structural role; the schools speak in different registers (S/AD/Eth) and to different addressees. Madhusūdana's *Bhakti-Rasāyana* (methodology Case 7) shows that the *jñānin* and the *bhakta* register can co-exist in one author; AF8 — two-register coherence does not collapse the AD vs. S distinction.

per_school.definition: Within Advaita, *bhakti* can be glossed as *svasvarūpānusandhāna*, an inwardly sustained orientation to the Self, while its outward comportment is reverential service to the guru ordered toward liberating inquiry [[VC 31](cite://sankara/vivekacudamani/31); [VC 34](cite://sankara/vivekacudamani/34)]. Later Advaita, in Madhusūdana's hands, gives the category an explicitly theistic and affective precision: *bhakti* is the mind's *bhagavad-ākāratā*, a *savikalpaka-vṛtti* preceded by *dravī-bhāva*, and is not reducible to *brahma-vidyā* [[BR 1.1 ṭīkā](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1)]. He further allows *bhakti-yoga* to be spoken of as a supreme *puruṣārtha* and as persisting even for the *jīvan-mukta* [[BR 1.1](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1); [BR 1.1 ṭīkā.20](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.20)].

Viśiṣṭādvaita treats *bhakti* as *upāsanā* of the supreme Person, preceded by *jīva-paramātma-yāthātmyajñāna* and by the *itikartavyatā* of *varṇāśrama-dharma*, with attainment of the Lord as its fruit [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3)]. The Vedāntic corpus is thus read as culminating not in bare cognition but in *tad-upāsanā* and in Brahman-experience preceded by the manifestation of the self's own nature [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. In Yāmuna's liturgical register, the same orientation contracts into *śaraṇāgati*: incapacity in *dharma*, self-knowledge, and devotion itself is confessed so that refuge at the Lord's feet becomes the operative form of dependence [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)].

Tattva-vāda makes *bhakti* inseparable from *vairāgya*, *upāsanā*, and Viṣṇu's gracious self-disclosure: firmness of devotion ripens into contemplative worship, and from that comes *aparokṣya* of Viṣṇu [[Anuvy. 3.3.1](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/3.3.1)]. Karma itself is to be undertaken only as preceded by *bhagavad-bhakti*, knowledge, and dispassion, so devotion is not a detachable ornament but part of the causal ordering of *sādhana* [[Karma-Nirṇaya p. 257a](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257a)]. Because Viṣṇu alone grants knowledge, liberation, and the bliss of the liberated, *bhakti* remains the *jīva*'s proper *paratantra* relation to the independent Lord rather than an episodic affect [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)].

Acintya-Bhedābheda defines *uttamā-bhakti* as *ānukūlyena kṛṣṇānuśīlana* free from alien desire and from the coverings of *jñāna* and *karma*; its admissibility-conditions are therefore both negative and positive [[BRS 1.1.11](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.11); [BRS 1.1.12](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.12)]. The school's developmental grammar then runs through *sādhana*, *bhāva*, and *prema*, with *bhakti-yoga* itself named the ultimate condition [[BRS 1.1.9](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.9); [BRS 1.1.15](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.15)]. In Caitanya's devotional idiom, its purified form is *ahaitukī bhakti* sought birth after birth, while Keśava Kāśmīri makes the same devotion the qualifying cause for relation to Hari and guru and the means by which mantra-practice reaches Murāri's pure abode [[Śikṣ 4](cite://caitanya/shikshashtakam/4); [KD 1.5](cite://kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/1.5); [KD 8.96](cite://kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96)].

*Bhakti* in Dvaitādvaita is born from the Lord's *kṛpā* in one furnished with *dainya* and allied dispositions, and its mature form is marked by *premaviśeṣa* rather than by mere instrumental religiosity [[DaŚ 9](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/9)]. Nimbārka accordingly distinguishes the higher devotion from the merely *sādhanarūpikā* lower form, and treats the savor of devotion as a further fruit of grace [[DaŚ 9](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/9); [DaŚ 10](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10)]. The school also places constant *upāsanā* of Hari and refuge at Kṛṣṇa's feet at the center of practice, so *bhakti* names an enduring *upāsaka-upāsya* relation rather than a transitory mood [[DaŚ 6](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/6); [DaŚ 8](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/8)].

Śuddhādvaita makes attainment of *Puruṣottama* depend on more than the removal of *avidyā*: *brahmajñāna* prepares *svarūpa-yogyatā*, but fruition requires divine *varaṇa* together with *bhakti-bhāva* as its *sahakāri-yogyatā* [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)]. Vallabha can therefore cite *bhaktyāham ekayā grāhyaḥ* from within the very account of Vedāntic attainment, making devotion internal to *prāpti* rather than external to it [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)]. Because the Lord is to be attained as Puruṣottama who chooses the soul as his own, *bhakti* functions here under the sign of grace and belonging, not as a merely self-generated exercise [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the available Sāṅkhya corpus, mokṣa is tied to tattvajñāna and the late upāsanā notices are ancillary or expressly non-liberating, so there is no developed Sāṅkhya doctrine of bhakti in the present sense.

per_school.definition: *Bhakti* enters the Yoga tradition through *īśvara-praṇidhāna*, which Vijñānabhikṣu refuses to reduce to *japa* alone and instead glosses as a distinctive cultivation of samādhi that becomes causal for *asaṃprajñāta* [[YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23)]. Its object is *Īśvara* as a *puruṣa-viśeṣa* untouched by *kleśa*, *karma*, *vipāka*, and *āśaya*, so devotional orientation is structurally tethered to Yoga's ontology of a unique Lord [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. In this register, *bhakti* is not a free-standing emotional category but a concentration-producing *praṇidhāna* with direct yogic efficacy [[YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the available Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika texts, bhakti appears only in semantic discussions of gauṇa or bhākta usage, while the school's soteriology is organized around tattvajñāna and apavarga rather than a developed doctrine of bhakti.
In the available Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā corpus, bhakti is a technical term for secondary signification or derivative application, not a devotional soteriological category.
The on-disk Mādhyamaka materials develop emptiness, two truths, bodhicitta, and mahākaruṇā, but they do not treat bhakti as a school-specific doctrinal category.
The on-disk Yogācāra materials develop vijñapti-mātra, śraddhā, kalyāṇamitra, and the path-structure of cognition, but not bhakti as a distinct doctrinal category.

per_school.definition: Within Trika, bhakti toward Śiva is not treated as an independently self-generated sentiment: when free of fruit-seeking it is identified with śaktipāta itself, whereas fruit-seeking bhakti still depends on karma and allied means [[AbhT 13.118-119](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/13.118-119)]. The tradition can therefore construe bhakti as the effect of prasāda and inner nirmalībhāva, by which Śiva becomes self-manifest in the adept and parā siddhi is attained [[AbhT 13.285-287](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/13.285-287)]. Within initiation and discipline, steady devotion to deity and guru accompanies the nirbīja-dīkṣā whose liberative efficacy is otherwise immediate [[AbhT 15.31](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/15.31)].

translator_note: English 'devotion' is often too affective and too inward, while 'faith' imports Protestant and creed-centered assumptions. *Bhakti* includes cognition, ritual performance, remembrance, service, and loving participation in relation to a real deity. Where the tradition uses the term technically, it does not mean vague religiosity or mere sentiment.
